Understand the market now and in the future. Look, let's spend a little time reminding you of where this market is, what this market looks like, why apartments are so valuable. People can't afford to buy homes today. Not only can they not afford to buy homes, they don't want to buy homes. They will become renters. We are becoming a renter nation. Let's spend a little time breaking that down. Where is the market right now? Are we at the top of the market as I sit here and shoot this content? Is the market overpriced? I hear every day, it's overpriced. We're at the top of the market. I've been told that for five or six years now and it influenced me in a negative way and caused me not to pull the trigger, not to purchase product apartments that I should have been buying. Is the market oversaturated with new inventory? New inventory meaning new build? Is the sellers in favor now? Is the buyer in favor? Look, all these are tough questions to answer. I can't predict or tell you where the market will be next week, next month, or next year. Every market is different. Your market could be very different than the market that I'm shopping in. And that's why it's so imperative for you to know your market, know your job and labor market, know the growth or the decline of the city to know what is needed in your market as far as a place to live. Again, buying apartments, creating wealth in real estate is not a hobby. This is not because you love real estate. It's because I love the idea that I can have a very simple business, supply people with a great place to live, to have their families, to provide them with a safe place to sleep at night and raise their families. And by taking care of them, I can get wealthy in the process. But you have to know your market and you must know your buying criteria. Here's a good example. A student came to me recently with a $1 million deal in Boston. Now, I don't know the Boston market. He shared with me that it was a three cap rate. That did not deter me even though three cap rate means this property is very expensive. The reason it did not deter me is because I know the Boston market, particularly with water views, a two, three, or 4% cap rate is probably what you're going to have to pay. And by the way, it will probably be a good deal if you have enough time. He shared with me that the property would make $30,000 a year. A $1 million investment would make $30,000 a year. But according to my calculations, he could almost double that if he used debt. If he financed the deal, he would have between $55,000 and $60,000 worth of cash flow, free cash flow by financing the deal. Instead of diving into the numbers, which I assume he'd expected I would do, I simply asked him if he could afford a pullback, meaning, hey, man, look, you only have $30,000 worth of cash flow. If you put debt on it, you would have $55,000 or $60,000. All you need to lose is one or two units, and you will be even like zero cash flow. Nobody makes investments for zero cash flow. Or you could even go negative. Can you afford a negative pullback? By the way, I'm telling you this because maybe I'm willing to have no cash flow. I once bought a property in Tucson, Arizona, and even though I'm a cash flow pig, I bought this property knowing it was not going to cash flow, but that I would make $2.2 million when I exited. I literally told my twin brother, we're not going to make any cash flow in this deal. I didn't take his money as an investor. It's going to be a flat, no cash back while I'm in it, but I'm going to probably make between $2 and $2.2 million. I made exactly $2.2 million on the exit, even though I didn't have any cash flow while I waited. Why did I do that? Because I had to include that property with the entire portfolio that I was buying. I bought 2200 units, almost 40 of them was this deal I didn't really want. It didn't have the cash flow, but I bought it because I wanted the rest of the portfolio. Look at some point in the market, something will happen. And it can be anything. It could be a massive economic shift, a national crisis. It could be a cyber attack and you can't collect your online payments. Are you in a position to go negative on your cash flow? Can you continue to take care of the debt or the property or both if you don't have the cash flow? In the case of the Tucson example that I gave you, I was in a position to do that. My other properties cash flowed enough to help me handle this one property that didn't. So that I could wait and profit. For the gentleman in Boston, by the way, he decided not to buy that property because it was his first deal and he was not in a position to go to a negative cash flow situation. So he continued to look in his market for deals that provided more cash flow. Look anything can happen. Financing rates could go up. Again, cyber attacks. Like who knows what's going to happen? A bunch of tenants move out. Head bugs hit your property and people demand to leave and create a bunch of havoc. All these things could impact an investment property and its cash flow. So I wondered, can you withstand days or weeks or even months? And in addition to this, are you built to handle the stress of no cash flow? And this is my big contention. I don't want stress or drama in my life around my finances. Another reason I don't invest in the stock market. Another reason I didn't invest in Bitcoin. Another reason I don't bet or gamble or take risk with my money. I don't want drama around my investing. Bad things happen and this is a long game. You need to be willing to invest over the long time to drive the appreciation that's going to give you the 2X and 3X and 4X return to drive meaningful increases from your initial investment. If you can weather 10 years like this, you'll be in good shape. Even if you buy at the top of the market, if you take a 10 year, 12 year horizon, you're going to find yourself at another top 10 or 12 years from now that you can sell into. Typically by the way, every 10 years apartment values have doubled in value in Florida. Think about that for a minute. Every 10 years, a $40,000 unit is now 80,000, 10 years later. 10 years after that, maybe another $160,000. If you look at the Florida market, that's pretty much what's happened over the last 20 to 30 years. What you need to know, as you can see, requires marketplace knowledge and knowledge of what you can withstand. I need to know both the market outside of me and I need to know what am I personally built for? How much stress can my system take? Can my finances take? And then, and then I'll know what to look at. Then I'll know what to pull the trigger on. Then I'll know, can I pay a little bit more than the other two people bidding on the property because I can withstand more? Also you need to know the cost to build in your market, especially if you're in a major metro market like a New York, a Boston, San Francisco, Chicago, Los Angeles, Denver. You need to know what is the cost to build. Even in other markets, tertiary markets, Tampa, Orlando, outside that, let's say closer to Daytona or St. Lucie, all that up along the coast of Florida, the northern coast, outside of Jacksonville, South. When you move into Savannah, tertiary market, what is the cost to build there? If I'm buying a property in South Savannah, I need to know what is it cost for people to build on the north side or in my backyard so I can have protection, barriers of entry. I need to know the cost to build even though I'm not a builder. Is it astronomical? Is it very affordable? What's the cost to plan? It's hard to nail down, but not if you do the research. I made a decision years ago when I was living in San Diego to invest in real estate and the reason I did is literally just the permit, check this out, just to permit one apartment 20 years ago in San Diego, California was $55,000 a door. I was buying the property for 80 grand a door, so 100 units would cost me about $8 million and it was a steal and everybody around me, they weren't buying it. Oh my God, we can't buy it now. Oh my God, they're talking about all the problems in the market and I'm like, it costs $55,000 to permit. We don't have lumber, no concrete, no glass, no building, $55,000. I'm buying this property for $80,000 a door. It's a steal and by the way, I was right. The mistake I made was never selling any of it. Look, this idea that they're going to build next door, they will if you bought a great location. You just need to know what the cost is, but you got to be aware. You got to be aware in markets where a lot of building is taking place. There's a saying, cranes in the air, beware. Builders always overbuild and builders almost always go broke. Okay, also you need to know what's the history of the market. If you were to go back to the absolute worst times in your market, what happened to this immediate market, to that building, to the rent there, to the occupancy? What pullback plans do you have? If that worst case scenario were to happen in your market, what would happen to you and your investment or you over-leveraged? Do you have too much debt? Do you have enough cash flow to weather that situation? Look for stress tests and what numbers can realistically get you to knowing that when there's a pullback, occupancy or vacancy really will drop so that you can look back and know that when there's a pullback, your occupancy will drop, your rental income will drop, your expenses will probably maintain or stay the same while your income got reduced. Can you handle it? Can you withstand it? And up to what level? For how long? By the way, is your debt maturing right at the time that the economy gets bad? This is when your DCR that we talked about earlier, your break-even rates come in to play in a big way. And that's why I always analyze my deals based on a worst case scenario. How long can I weather the storm? Understanding occupancy. What does occupancy look like typically and what does it look like in a worst case moment? What is your NOI at 100% occupancy and is 100% occupancy even possible? It's not. What about at 90, 85? Less than that. You use a break-even number in your analyzing so you get to see, oh, at 82, I'm breaking even on my deal and this property has never been below 91. That would be a good indicator. What do these dips do for other incoming revenue applications like the fees and the parking and the laundry and deposits? What's it going to do to your neighbor across the street that maybe didn't put enough money down or raised at all from investors, has no money in the deal, took out a bad loan? What's it going to do to him? Is it going to squeeze him off? Maybe you'll be a buyer for his property. Know everything. Rents, rates and supply. What new products coming on the market? What are rents in your market? What are the rates? Even if you've been in a property for 20 or 25 years, because your cost is so low in that property, you don't have to raise the rents. But if everybody around you is raising rents, then you have an obligation to make sure that you do the same so that you get the maximum return on your investment. Let's say you can borrow money right now at 4%. Some of that money is interest only for 5 to 10 years. The longer you can get that interest only period, the more you can afford to invest. So you need to know, is interest only loans available in my market? Will Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac or your local bank or regional bank or even an insurance company provide interest only in your market or you're 120 miles away from that ideal market for interest only? What I'm saying to you is this, the banks might lend money 5 or 10 years of interest only in Orlando because there's so much job growth there. But if you just go 120 miles away or even 80 miles away to Daytona, they're like, no, we won't do that there. We'll only do 3 years. Know your market. The longer you can get interest only period, the more you can afford to invest in the actual property and improving the property and paying your investors back. Interest only payments again are significantly less than regular interest and principal payments because you're not paying the principal down. You paid it down in advance. Again eliminate the principal payment and you suddenly have a bigger immediate impact to your cash flow. If you're paying cash for a property, don't know why you would but let's say you did. Let's say a property with a 4% cap rate, that's very different than an interest rate. The 4% cap rate means a 4 cap on a million dollar property would pay me $40,000. Too many first time investors confuse the interest rate with the cap rate. Know them. Both are important numbers that impact your deals even if you're not financing your deal. Why? Because when you go to sell the deal, the next buyer is probably going to use financing. Interest impacts how much you can afford. So at this stage in the cycle, it's important to understand and apply interest rates, cap rates and all the other information that you're collecting. Keep in mind banks and lenders worry. They worry and they should worry. This is why things like PEDA, principal interest, taxes and insurance withholding exists. The lender will actually withhold your principal, your interest, your taxes and your insurance for you to make sure they're handled. I don't like that. They're called lock boxes or lender controls. I don't want the lender controlling my income, but maybe you're just getting started. I've barred $400, $500 million from the banks and Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and life insurance companies. They trust me. They normally don't trust anyone, but once you've proven out what you can do and what you will do and you're a good operator, they'll trust you too. Like you, these lenders have lived through the housing crisis. They know what can happen. Be conservative with your estimates. They will help you stay conservative and the bank is your friend. They will help make sure you don't get yourself in trouble. Also, I want to talk to you again about how younger buyers, millennials particularly are impacting the apartment market. Within that rent, rates and supply consideration, there's one other note that's unique at this moment in time. For instance, millennials, the younger buyers right now are pushing off their home purchase. They're getting married later, having families later, and they appear to not be as interested in buying a home. Maybe they can't afford the down payment necessary. Maybe it's because of the student loan. Maybe they're reluctant to leave the city they're in. Maybe they're new to a city or they don't trust their job. Maybe they just don't want to own a home like their parents did. Maybe they watch their mom and dad lose their home and all the equity and be underwater for five or six or seven years. Maybe things are just changing. They're getting married later. They're having kids later. Maybe they just don't want to buy. Maybe they want mobility that I talk about to people all the time. To be mobile, be able to move. Regardless of the reason, this is positively impacting the apartment market and I believe will continue to do so well into 2030. For investors, that's positive. Rents will continue to go up. Know your market.
